---
title: "Market Definition and Competitive Landscape"
---

## Learning goals
- Frame relevant product and geographic markets using demand evidence, diversion, and qualitative industry facts.
- Apply and critique common screens (SSNIP logic, critical loss, diversion ratios).
- Blend survey evidence, internal documents, and empirical estimates into a coherent narrative.

## Core tools
- Price and quantity descriptive analytics; seasonality and shocks.
- Elasticity estimation (log-log, AIDS-lite) and diversion from switching/choice data.
- Critical loss vs. actual loss calculations; pitfalls with margins and capacity constraints.
- Switching matrices from customer data; shipment flows for geography.

::: {.callout-note title="Method box"}
- Quick diversion calc from transaction data.
- Event studies for entry/exit shocks informing market scope.
- Cross-price elasticity estimation; bootstrapping confidence intervals.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Qualitative evidence"}
- Product positioning from marketing decks and sales playbooks.
- Customer interviews/surveys on substitutes and procurement processes.
- Bids/RFP language showing substitutability and switching costs.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Case box"}
- Market definition debates in hospital services (narrow geography vs. insurer bargaining).
- Tech platform self-preferencing and the role of default position evidence.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Citations and comparative note"}
- Cite market definition sources such as US Merger Guidelines SSNIP discussion [@doj_ftc_hmg_2010] and EC Notice on market definition [@ec_market_definition_2024]; add CMA cases for UK context.
- When using surveys or switching analyses, reference standards on survey reliability (e.g., reference guides, cases admitting/excluding surveys).
- Flag jurisdictional differences explicitly when SSNDQ or platform-centric approaches apply more than SSNIP (e.g., EU digital contexts).
:::
